meta.sponsor: Russian Foreign Intelligence Service (SVR) — per U.S. Government formal attribution, April 2021
meta.confidence: high — confirmed by FireEye/Mandiant, Microsoft MSTIC, CrowdStrike, Volexity, CISA, and the U.S. Government's joint intelligence community statement
meta.first_observed: malicious code inserted into Orion builds September 2019; trojanized updates distributed March-June 2020
meta.last_observed: publicly disclosed December 13, 2020; remediation and follow-on Nobelium activity tracked into 2021
meta.status: disclosed and remediated at scale; threat actor (APT29) remains active under continued tracking as Nobelium/Midnight Blizzard in later reporting
meta.motivation: state-sponsored espionage — intelligence collection against U.S. government agencies, government contractors, think tanks, and technology companies
meta.target_sectors: U.S. federal government (Treasury, Commerce/NTIA, State, DHS/CISA, DOJ, NIH, DOE/NNSA), technology vendors (Microsoft, Cisco, Intel, Nvidia, VMware, Belkin), cybersecurity firms (FireEye), think tanks (CSIS)
meta.target_geography: primarily United States federal agencies and U.S.-headquartered technology/security companies; approximately 18,000 organizations worldwide received the trojanized Orion update, with a much smaller number selected for hands-on-keyboard follow-on activity
meta.associated_tools: SUNSPOT (build-server implant), SUNBURST (trojanized Orion DLL backdoor), TEARDROP and RAINDROP (memory-only Cobalt Strike loaders), GoldMax/GoldFinder/Sibot (secondary Nobelium backdoors), forged SAML authentication tokens ("Golden SAML"), a stolen SolarWinds code-signing certificate
meta.summary: Russian SVR-attributed threat actor APT29 compromised the build system for SolarWinds' Orion IT-monitoring platform, inserting the SUNSPOT implant to inject a backdoor (SUNBURST) into official, digitally-signed software updates distributed to roughly 18,000 SolarWinds customers between March and June 2020. SUNBURST called out to attacker-controlled infrastructure (avsvmcloud[.]com) after a dormancy period of up to two weeks, and only a small, carefully selected subset of victims received hands-on-keyboard follow-on tooling (TEARDROP, GoldMax, forged SAML tokens) for deeper access. FireEye discovered the operation in December 2020 after detecting the theft of its own Red Team assessment tools, and disclosed it publicly alongside SolarWinds on December 13, 2020. CISA issued Emergency Directive 21-01 ordering federal civilian agencies to disconnect affected Orion instances. The U.S. Government formally attributed the operation to Russia's SVR in April 2021 and imposed sanctions in response.
meta.source: FireEye/Mandiant ("Highly Evasive Attacker Leverages SolarWinds Supply Chain to Compromise Multiple Global Victims", Dec 2020); Microsoft MSTIC ("Analyzing Solorigate" blog series); CrowdStrike ("SUNSPOT" analysis); Volexity ("Dark Halo" reporting); CISA Emergency Directive 21-01; U.S. White House/ODNI joint statement, April 15, 2021

meta.aliases: Cozy Bear, The Dukes, UNC2452, Nobelium, StellarParticle, Dark Halo, Midnight Blizzard
meta.status: active, continues to be tracked in follow-on campaigns
meta.summary: A long-tracked Russian state-sponsored espionage group assessed to operate on behalf of the SVR, publicly linked to the 2015-16 DNC intrusion and, per U.S. Government attribution, the SolarWinds/SUNBURST supply chain operation. Distinguished by disciplined operational security, minimal footprint, and selective follow-on targeting of high-value victims out of a much larger pool of initial access.

Monitored the SolarWinds Orion build server for compile jobs and silently swapped in a trojanized source file, injecting the SUNBURST backdoor during the legitimate build process so the resulting binary carried a valid SolarWinds code-signing signature.
meta.source: CrowdStrike, "SUNSPOT: An Implant in the Build Process", January 2021

meta.summary: A backdoor embedded in SolarWinds.Orion.Core.BusinessLayer.dll, digitally signed with a legitimate SolarWinds certificate and distributed through routine Orion platform updates. Remained dormant for up to two weeks before beaconing to attacker-controlled infrastructure disguised as Orion Improvement Program traffic, and only escalated to hands-on-keyboard access on a small, deliberately selected subset of the ~18,000 organizations that installed it.
A custom, memory-resident dropper deployed via SUNBURST on selected high-value victims to load a customized Cobalt Strike Beacon directly into memory, leaving minimal forensic artifacts on disk.

A loader functionally similar to TEARDROP, also used to deploy Cobalt Strike on a subset of compromised networks; unlike TEARDROP it was found being used for lateral spread rather than delivered directly by SUNBURST.

A Golang-based command-and-control backdoor used on a subset of Nobelium victim networks, engineered to blend its traffic patterns with legitimate activity and disguise itself from network-based detection.
A custom HTTP-tracing tool likely used to log the route and servers/proxies traffic traversed to reach a C2 endpoint, helping the operators understand and evade network defenses.
A dual-purpose VBScript-based tool used both to maintain persistence on compromised machines and to download and execute a payload from a remote C2 server.

meta.description: Manipulated the SolarWinds Orion build environment so that officially released, digitally-signed software updates carried a backdoor, converting one vendor's routine update mechanism into a mass-distribution channel.
SUNSPOT modified source code during the build process so the resulting compiled Orion binary itself contained the SUNBURST backdoor, rather than patching a binary after the fact.
The trojanized Orion DLL was signed with a legitimate SolarWinds code-signing certificate, inheriting the trust that signature conferred and defeating signature-based allowlisting.
SUNBURST's network traffic and file/process naming were deliberately designed to resemble legitimate Orion Improvement Program telemetry, blending into expected product behavior.
SUNBURST remained dormant for up to two weeks after installation before its first callback, evading detection windows and sandboxed analysis that typically runs for only minutes to hours.

On select victim networks, the actor compromised on-premises identity infrastructure to steal the private key used to sign SAML tokens, then forged tokens to impersonate any user — including with MFA enabled — for federated cloud services such as Microsoft 365 (the "Golden SAML" technique).

Forged authentication tokens and compromised credentials were used to access cloud resources (email, files, cloud administration) as legitimate users, avoiding the need for further malware deployment in the cloud environment.
SUNBURST generated subdomains of avsvmcloud[.]com algorithmically based on victim-specific identifiers, complicating static blocklisting and helping the C2 channel blend into ordinary DNS traffic.
Initial beaconing and tasking used DNS requests to attacker infrastructure, a protocol rarely inspected as closely as HTTP/HTTPS in many network environments at the time.
On selected high-value victims, SUNBURST retrieved and executed TEARDROP, which in turn loaded a customized Cobalt Strike Beacon for hands-on-keyboard follow-on access.

entities.label: CVE-2020-10148 — SolarWinds Orion API Authentication Bypass

meta.summary: An authentication bypass in the SolarWinds Orion API allowing remote execution of API commands, disclosed alongside the SUNBURST investigation; usable by an attacker who had already gained network access to further interact with an Orion deployment.

The vendor whose Orion IT-monitoring build environment was compromised, turning its own trusted software-update mechanism into the initial distribution channel for SUNBURST to roughly 18,000 customers.

Cybersecurity firm that discovered it had been breached (including theft of its Red Team assessment tools) in early December 2020, traced the intrusion to a trojanized SolarWinds Orion update, and publicly disclosed the supply chain compromise on December 13, 2020.

meta.status: victim; also led significant public research (MSTIC)
meta.summary: Confirmed as a victim of the SUNBURST intrusion and, through its Microsoft Threat Intelligence Center (MSTIC), published extensive follow-on research on Nobelium tooling including GoldMax, GoldFinder, and Sibot.
One of the first U.S. federal agencies confirmed to have been breached via the SUNBURST backdoor, reported in initial December 2020 disclosures.
The National Telecommunications and Information Administration, part of the Department of Commerce, confirmed as breached with attacker access observed to internal email systems.
Reported among the federal agencies compromised via the Orion supply chain intrusion.

meta.status: victim and lead federal coordinator for the response
meta.summary: The Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency, itself among the affected agencies, issued Emergency Directive 21-01 ordering federal civilian agencies to disconnect or patch affected Orion installations and coordinated the broader federal incident response.

The Department of Energy and its National Nuclear Security Administration confirmed malware had been found on their networks, though officials stated no impact to national security functions including the nuclear weapons stockpile.
Confirmed that a portion of its Microsoft O365 email environment had been accessed, affecting a small percentage of DOJ email accounts.
Reported among the federal agencies running compromised Orion software during the campaign window.

meta.status: confirmed to have run the trojanized update
meta.summary: Confirmed it had installed the compromised Orion update, and published its own incident response findings.

meta.status: reported to have run the trojanized update
meta.summary: Reported among the technology companies that had installed the compromised Orion update.

meta.status: reported to have run the trojanized update
meta.summary: Reported among the technology companies that had installed the compromised Orion update.

meta.status: reported to have run the trojanized update
meta.summary: Reported among the technology companies that had installed the compromised Orion update; separately disclosed unrelated vulnerabilities exploited by the same actor in follow-on activity.

entities.label: Center for Strategic and International Studies (CSIS)

meta.summary: A Washington think tank reported among the non-governmental organizations targeted, consistent with APT29's historical interest in policy research institutions.
Discovered the SUNBURST backdoor while investigating the theft of its own Red Team tools, and led the initial public technical disclosure on December 13, 2020.
Published extensive follow-on research identifying additional Nobelium tooling (GoldMax, GoldFinder, Sibot) used on a subset of victim networks after initial SUNBURST access.
Identified and published analysis of SUNSPOT, the build-server implant used to inject SUNBURST into the Orion source code during compilation.
Had independently observed related "Dark Halo" intrusion activity at a U.S. think tank as early as December 2019, later connected to the same actor behind SUNBURST.

Issued Emergency Directive 21-01 on December 13, 2020 ordering all federal civilian executive branch agencies to disconnect or patch affected SolarWinds Orion instances, and coordinated the multi-agency Cyber Unified Coordination Group response with the FBI, ODNI, and NSA.
